title: Writing a Research Paper - Multiple Response

questions.question: Which elements must appear in EVERY introduction, according to Wallwork? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Define the topic and give background
options.B: State the accepted state and the problem
options.C: Give the authors' objectives or aim

questions.explanation: All introductions define the topic/background, state the accepted state plus the problem, give the aim, and introduce the literature. Reporting numerical results (E) belongs in the Results section, not the introduction.

questions.question: Which signal an INFORMAL register that should be avoided in an introduction? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Rhetorical questions aimed at the reader

questions.explanation: Contractions, rhetorical questions, sentence-initial And/But/So, and vague approximations or slang all mark an informal register to avoid. A clearly stated aim (E) is required content, not an informality.

questions.question: In CARS, which moves can be used to establish a niche (Move 2)? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Challenge earlier work with a counterclaim
options.B: Indicate a gap in what is known
options.C: Extend prior research in a new direction
options.D: Review what is already known about the topic
options.E: State the paper's specific purpose and structure

questions.explanation: Move 2 establishes a niche by counterclaim, gap, or extension of prior work. Reviewing what is known (D) is Move 1, and stating the purpose and structure (E) is Move 3.

questions.question: Which subsections are typical of a participant-study Methods section? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: A participant study typically runs Participants, Materials/Stimuli, Design, and Procedure (often with Aim and Analysis). Dataset and Preprocessing (E) belong to the ML-study Methods order, not the participant-study order.

questions.question: What must a Results section EXCLUDE? (Select all that apply)

options.E: A summary of the main patterns found

questions.explanation: Results excludes interpretation, raw scores, background, procedure, stats-class explanations, and irrelevant data. A summary of the main patterns (E) is exactly what Results should include.

questions.question: Which requirements apply to tables and figures in a Results section? (Select all that apply)

options.A: They must be referred to in the text
options.B: They must be understandable on their own
options.C: They must not merely repeat the prose
options.D: They must replace the prose summary entirely
options.E: They must list every individual data point

questions.explanation: Exhibits must be referenced in text, be independent (understandable alone), and not duplicate the prose. They do not replace the prose summary (D), and the text should report patterns rather than every point (E).

questions.question: Which prompts make up the 'message in a bottle' test you apply before writing the Discussion? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Why did you do the study?
options.C: What are the main findings, and why should people care?
options.D: What is new about the work?
options.E: Which font and citation style will the journal require?

questions.explanation: The message-in-a-bottle test asks why you did the study, the central question, the main findings, why people should care, and what is new, distilled to one core sentence. Formatting choices like font or citation style (E) are not part of this test.

questions.question: Why do academic writers hedge? (Select all that apply)

options.A: To reduce the risk of overstating a claim
options.B: To be more precise about uncertainty
options.D: To hide weaknesses in the data

questions.explanation: Hedging reduces risk, improves precision, and meets academic expectations, using tools such as UEFAP and the Manchester Academic Phrasebank. It is not used to hide weaknesses (D); limitations should be stated honestly.

questions.question: Which belong in the abstract's five required elements? (Select all that apply)

options.C: Study type, design, and materials or participants

questions.explanation: The abstract maps onto IMRaD with topic/background, RQ/aim, study type/design/materials, main outcome, and conclusion/implications. A reference list (E) is never part of an abstract.

questions.question: Which statements about the known-new contract and abstract style are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Each sentence should start from what is known and introduce something new
options.B: Parts are linked with signposts such as 'Additionally' or 'Together, these results'
options.C: The abstract is a connected paragraph, not a bulleted list
options.D: Choppy, disconnected sentences are preferred for clarity

questions.explanation: The known-new contract links sentences from known to new with signposts, producing a connected paragraph. Choppy, disconnected sentences (D) are explicitly to be avoided in the abstract.
